title: Hash Map
description: A hash map (dict in Python, map in Go) stores key-value pairs with average O(1) lookup, insert, and delete. It uses a hash function to compute an index into an array of buckets.
diagram:
Key  →  hash(key) % size  →  bucket index

  "apple" → 3 → [3]: ("apple", 5)
  "grape" → 7 → [7]: ("grape", 12)
  "pear"  → 3 → [3]: collision → chaining

Lookup:  O(1) avg,  O(n) worst
Insert:  O(1) avg
Delete:  O(1) avg
keyPoints: Average O(1) for get/set/delete
Collisions handled by chaining or open addressing
Load factor triggers resize (typically at 0.75)
Keys must be hashable (immutable in Python)
Common pattern: counting frequencies, caching, grouping

solutions.python:
# Frequency count
nums = [1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3]
freq = {}
for n in nums:
    freq[n] = freq.get(n, 0) + 1
# freq = {1:1, 2:2, 3:3}

# Using Counter
from collections import Counter
freq = Counter(nums)

# defaultdict
from collections import defaultdict
graph = defaultdict(list)
graph['a'].append('b')

title: Binary Search
description: Binary search finds a target in a sorted array in O(log n) time by repeatedly halving the search space. Key insight: if the middle element isn't the target, you can eliminate half the array.
diagram:
Array: [-1, 0, 3, 5, 9, 12]  target = 9
         0   1  2  3  4   5

Step 1:  L=0, R=5  mid=2  nums[2]=3 < 9  → L=3
Step 2:  L=3, R=5  mid=4  nums[4]=9 = 9  → return 4 ✓

Invariant: target is always in [L, R]
Loop condition: L <= R
keyPoints: Array MUST be sorted (or have a sorted property)
Use mid = left + (right - left) // 2 to avoid overflow
Three cases: found, go left (right = mid-1), go right (left = mid+1)
Template: while left <= right (inclusive bounds)
Common trick: binary search on the answer space, not just an array

title: Sliding Window
description: Sliding window maintains a window (subarray/substring) using two pointers and slides it across the input. Used for problems asking for optimal contiguous subarray/substring with some constraint.
diagram:
Find max sum subarray of size k=3:
[2, 1, 5, 1, 3, 2]

[2  1  5] 1  3  2   sum=8
 2 [1  5  1] 3  2   sum=7
 2  1 [5  1  3] 2   sum=9  ← max
 2  1  5 [1  3  2]  sum=6

Window slides: remove left, add right → O(n)
keyPoints: Fixed window: move both pointers together
Variable window: expand right, shrink left when constraint violated
Use for: max/min subarray, longest substring, anagram detection
Track window state with a counter/map/sum
Time: O(n) — each element enters and exits window once

solutions.python:
# Fixed window — max sum of size k
def max_sum_fixed(nums, k):
    window_sum = sum(nums[:k])
    best = window_sum
    for i in range(k, len(nums)):
        window_sum += nums[i] - nums[i - k]
        best = max(best, window_sum)
    return best

# Variable window — longest substring without repeating
def length_of_longest_substring(s):
    seen = set()
    left = best = 0
    for right, ch in enumerate(s):
        while ch in seen:
            seen.remove(s[left])
            left += 1
        seen.add(ch)
        best = max(best, right - left + 1)
    return best

title: DFS & BFS
description: DFS (Depth-First Search) explores as far as possible before backtracking — uses a stack (or recursion). BFS (Breadth-First Search) explores level by level — uses a queue. Both visit all nodes in O(V+E).
diagram:
Graph:
  1 → 2 → 4
  1 → 3 → 5

DFS (stack): 1 → 2 → 4 → 3 → 5  (go deep first)
BFS (queue): 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5  (level by level)

When to use:
  DFS → path existence, cycles, topological sort
  BFS → shortest path (unweighted), level order
keyPoints: DFS: use recursion or explicit stack
BFS: always use a queue (deque)
Always track visited nodes to avoid infinite loops
BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs
DFS space: O(height), BFS space: O(width)

title: Two Pointers
description: Two pointers uses two indices that move through the data structure, often from both ends toward the middle or at different speeds. Reduces many O(n²) brute force problems to O(n).
diagram:
Opposite direction (sorted array sum):
Target = 9:  [1, 3, 5, 7, 9]
              L           R   sum=10 > 9 → R--
              L        R      sum=8  < 9 → L++
              L     R         sum=8  < 9 → L++
                 L  R         sum=10 > 9 → R--
                 LR           L==R → stop

Same direction (fast/slow — cycle detection):
  slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps
keyPoints: Opposite ends: sort first, then converge (pair sum, palindrome check)
Same direction: one slow + one fast pointer (remove duplicates, cycle detection)
Requires sorted array for most opposite-direction patterns
Floyd's cycle detection: fast/slow pointers meet if there's a cycle
Time: O(n), Space: O(1)

solutions.python:
# Two Sum in sorted array
def two_sum_sorted(nums, target):
    left, right = 0, len(nums) - 1
    while left < right:
        s = nums[left] + nums[right]
        if s == target: return [left, right]
        elif s < target: left += 1
        else: right -= 1
    return []

# Remove duplicates in-place
def remove_duplicates(nums):
    k = 1
    for i in range(1, len(nums)):
        if nums[i] != nums[i-1]:
            nums[k] = nums[i]
            k += 1
    return k

# Detect cycle (Floyd's)
def has_cycle(head):
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
        if slow == fast: return True
    return False

title: Sets
description: A set stores unique elements with average O(1) add, remove, and lookup. No duplicates, no guaranteed order (except LinkedHashSet / sorted variants). Python has set, Go uses map[T]struct{}, Java has HashSet/TreeSet.
keyPoints: O(1) average for add / remove / contains
Go has no built-in set — use map[T]struct{} (zero memory value)
Python set elements must be hashable (no lists as elements)
Java HashSet = unordered, TreeSet = sorted O(log n)
Common use: deduplication, visited tracking, O(1) membership test

title: Rune & String Types
description: In Go, strings are immutable byte slices (UTF-8 encoded). A rune is an alias for int32 representing a Unicode code point. Iterating with range gives runes (correct for Unicode); indexing gives bytes. Python has no separate char type — strings are sequences of Unicode chars. Java uses char (UTF-16) and Character.
diagram:
Go string internals:
  s := "hello"
  s[0]         → byte  104  ('h')
  []rune(s)[0] → rune  104  ('h')

  s := "café"
  len(s)         → 5  (bytes, 'é' = 2 bytes)
  len([]rune(s)) → 4  (characters)

  range iterates runes:
  for i, r := range "café" { ... }
  // i=0 'c', i=1 'a', i=2 'f', i=3 'é'
keyPoints: Go: rune = int32, byte = uint8. Use rune for Unicode-safe char ops
Go: len(s) = byte count, len([]rune(s)) = character count
Go: range over string yields (byte index, rune) pairs
Python: str is Unicode by default, no separate char type. ord('a')=97, chr(97)='a'
Java: char is UTF-16 (2 bytes), Character has utility methods

solutions.python:
s = "hello"

# No char type — single char is a length-1 string
ch = s[0]          # 'h'
ord('h')           # 104  (Unicode code point)
chr(104)           # 'h'

# String → list of chars
chars = list(s)    # ['h','e','l','l','o']
''.join(chars)     # 'hello'

# Check char type
'a'.isalpha()      # True
'3'.isdigit()      # True
'a'.isupper()      # False
'A'.lower()        # 'a'

# Iterate
for ch in "café":
    print(ch)      # c a f é  (Unicode-safe)

title: Bit Manipulation
description: Bit manipulation operates directly on integer bits using bitwise operators. Essential for low-level optimization, flag masking, and many LeetCode problems. Works identically in Python, Go, and Java.
diagram:
Operators (integer level):
  a & b   AND   — 1 only if both 1
  a | b   OR    — 1 if either is 1
  a ^ b   XOR   — 1 if bits differ
  ~a      NOT   — flip all bits
  a << n  Left  — multiply by 2^n
  a >> n  Right — divide by 2^n

Common masks (for bit i):
  mask = 1 << i
  Get i-th:    (n >> i) & 1
  Set i-th:    n | mask
  Clear i-th:  n & ~mask
  Toggle i-th: n ^ mask
keyPoints: x & (x-1)  removes the lowest set bit — use to count set bits
x & (-x) isolates the lowest set bit
x ^ x = 0  and  x ^ 0 = x — XOR to find unpaired element
Left shift << n  =  multiply by 2^n
Right shift >> n  =  divide by 2^n (arithmetic in Java/Go, logical in Python)

solutions.python:
n = 0b1011   # 11 in binary

# Operators
print(n & 0b1010)   # AND  → 0b1010 = 10
print(n | 0b0100)   # OR   → 0b1111 = 15
print(n ^ 0b1001)   # XOR  → 0b0010 = 2
print(~n)           # NOT  → -12 (two's complement)
print(n << 1)       # Left shift  → 22
print(n >> 1)       # Right shift → 5

i = 2  # operate on bit 2
mask = 1 << i

# Get i-th bit
(n >> i) & 1          # 0 or 1

# Set i-th bit (force to 1)
n | mask

# Clear i-th bit (force to 0)
n & ~mask

# Toggle i-th bit (flip)
n ^ mask

# Count set bits
bin(n).count('1')     # Pythonic
int.bit_count(n)      # Python 3.10+

# Check power of 2
n > 0 and (n & (n-1)) == 0

# Classic XOR trick: find single unpaired number
nums = [1, 2, 2, 3, 3]
result = 0
for x in nums: result ^= x
# result == 1

title: Stack
description: A stack is a LIFO (Last In, First Out) structure. Push adds to the top, pop removes from the top. Peek/look reads the top without removing it. Used for undo, call stack, balanced parens, monotonic problems.
keyPoints: LIFO — last element pushed is first to be popped
Peek (look) reads the top element without removing it
Use a list/slice/deque as the underlying container
Common uses: balanced parentheses, DFS, next greater element, undo
Monotonic stack: keep elements in increasing/decreasing order for range problems

solutions.python:
# Python list as stack
stack = []

stack.append(1)   # push
stack.append(2)
stack.append(3)

top = stack[-1]   # peek / look — O(1), no removal
print(top)        # 3

val = stack.pop() # pop — removes and returns top
print(val)        # 3

print(len(stack)) # 2
print(not stack)  # False (non-empty)

# Using collections.deque (thread-safe, same API)
from collections import deque
stack = deque()
stack.append(4)   # push
stack[-1]         # peek
stack.pop()       # pop

# Monotonic (decreasing) stack example
def next_greater(nums):
    res = [-1] * len(nums)
    stack = []          # stores indices
    for i, n in enumerate(nums):
        while stack and nums[stack[-1]] < n:
            res[stack.pop()] = n
        stack.append(i)
    return res

title: Queue
description: A queue is a FIFO (First In, First Out) structure. Enqueue adds to the back, dequeue removes from the front. Essential for BFS, level-order traversal, and sliding window problems.
diagram:
enqueue(4)            dequeue()
        ↓                    ↑
  back → [ 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 ] → front

  enqueue: O(1)   dequeue: O(1)
  peek front: O(1)
keyPoints: FIFO — first element enqueued is first dequeued
Use deque for O(1) both ends (list.pop(0) is O(n) in Python)
BFS always uses a queue — level-order traversal, shortest path
Double-ended queue (deque) supports O(1) push/pop from both ends
Priority queue (heap) dequeues by priority, not insertion order

solutions.python:
from collections import deque

queue = deque()

# enqueue (add to back)
queue.append(1)
queue.append(2)
queue.append(3)

# peek front
front = queue[0]      # 1, no removal

# dequeue (remove from front)
val = queue.popleft() # 1

print(len(queue))     # 2
print(not queue)      # False

# Double-ended: add/remove both sides
queue.appendleft(0)   # add to front
queue.pop()           # remove from back

# BFS template
from collections import deque
def bfs(graph, start):
    visited = {start}
    queue = deque([start])
    while queue:
        node = queue.popleft()
        for nei in graph[node]:
            if nei not in visited:
                visited.add(nei)
                queue.append(nei)

# Priority queue (min-heap)
import heapq
pq = []
heapq.heappush(pq, (priority, value))
item = heapq.heappop(pq)  # smallest priority first

title: Linked List & Nodes
description: A linked list is a chain of nodes where each node stores a value and a pointer to the next node. No random access — traversal is O(n). Insertion/deletion at a known node is O(1). Key patterns: two pointers, dummy head, reverse in-place.
diagram:
head
   ↓
  [1] → [2] → [3] → [4] → None

  Node: { val, next }
  Doubly: { val, prev, next }

  Insert after node p:    O(1)
  Delete node (given):    O(1)
  Find node by value:     O(n)
  Access by index:        O(n)
keyPoints: Always check for None/null before accessing .next
Dummy head node eliminates edge cases at the list head
Slow/fast pointer (Floyd's): cycle detection, find middle
Reverse: keep prev, curr, next pointers and re-link
Doubly linked list: O(1) delete given the node itself (no need to find prev)

solutions.python:
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val
        self.next = next

# Build list: 1 → 2 → 3
head = ListNode(1, ListNode(2, ListNode(3)))

# Traverse
curr = head
while curr:
    print(curr.val)
    curr = curr.next

# Insert after node p
def insert_after(p, val):
    new_node = ListNode(val, p.next)
    p.next = new_node

# Delete next node of p
def delete_next(p):
    if p.next:
        p.next = p.next.next

# Reverse linked list (iterative)
def reverse(head):
    prev, curr = None, head
    while curr:
        nxt = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = nxt
    return prev  # new head

# Find middle (slow/fast)
def find_middle(head):
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
    return slow

# Detect cycle
def has_cycle(head):
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
        if slow is fast:
            return True
    return False

title: Sorting
description: Sorting rearranges elements in order. Built-in sorts use Timsort (Python/Java) or pdqsort (Go) — both O(n log n). Knowing when to use a custom comparator and understanding stability matters for interviews.
diagram:
Algorithm   Time (avg)  Space  Stable?
Timsort     O(n log n)  O(n)   Yes  ← Python, Java
pdqsort     O(n log n)  O(log) No   ← Go
Merge sort  O(n log n)  O(n)   Yes
Quick sort  O(n log n)  O(log) No
Heap sort   O(n log n)  O(1)   No
Counting    O(n+k)      O(k)   Yes  ← small int range
keyPoints: Default sort is O(n log n) in all three languages
Python sort is stable; Go's sort.Slice is NOT stable (use sort.SliceStable)
Custom key/comparator: Python key=lambda, Go less func, Java Comparator
Counting sort beats O(n log n) when range k is small
Sorted order enables binary search, two-pointer, and greedy approaches

solutions.python:
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]

# Sort in-place
nums.sort()                      # ascending
nums.sort(reverse=True)          # descending

# Return new sorted list
sorted_nums = sorted(nums)

# Custom key — sort by absolute value
nums.sort(key=lambda x: abs(x))

# Sort list of tuples/objects
people = [("Alice", 30), ("Bob", 25), ("Carol", 35)]
people.sort(key=lambda p: p[1])  # by age

# Sort strings by length
words = ["banana", "apple", "kiwi"]
words.sort(key=len)

# Reverse a list
nums.reverse()          # in-place
rev = nums[::-1]        # new list

# Counting sort (small integer range)
def counting_sort(nums, max_val):
    count = [0] * (max_val + 1)
    for n in nums: count[n] += 1
    return [n for n, c in enumerate(count) for _ in range(c)]

title: Arrays — Operations
description: Arrays (lists in Python, slices in Go) are contiguous memory blocks with O(1) random access. Key operations: append, insert, delete, slice, reverse, search. Understanding underlying costs prevents hidden O(n) bugs.
diagram:
Index:  0    1    2    3    4
          [10] [20] [30] [40] [50]

  Random access:  arr[i]       O(1)
  Append to end:               O(1) amortized
  Insert at index i:           O(n)  ← shifts right
  Delete at index i:           O(n)  ← shifts left
  Search (unsorted):           O(n)
  Search (sorted + bin search):O(log n)
keyPoints: O(1) access by index, O(n) insert/delete in the middle
Python list / Go slice grow dynamically (amortized O(1) append)
Slicing creates a view (Go) or a copy (Python)
Two-pointer and sliding window patterns avoid nested loops
Prefix sums convert range-sum queries from O(n) to O(1)

solutions.python:
arr = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]

# Access
print(arr[0])       # 10
print(arr[-1])      # 50 (last)

# Slice (returns copy)
print(arr[1:4])     # [20, 30, 40]
print(arr[::-1])    # [50, 40, 30, 20, 10]  reversed copy

# Append / extend
arr.append(60)               # O(1) amortized
arr.extend([70, 80])         # add multiple

# Insert at index (O(n))
arr.insert(2, 25)            # insert 25 at index 2

# Remove
arr.pop()                    # remove last — O(1)
arr.pop(2)                   # remove at index — O(n)
arr.remove(25)               # remove first occurrence — O(n)

# Search
idx = arr.index(30)          # O(n)
print(30 in arr)             # O(n)

# Reverse in-place
arr.reverse()                # O(n)

# Prefix sum
def prefix_sum(nums):
    prefix = [0] * (len(nums) + 1)
    for i, n in enumerate(nums):
        prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + n
    return prefix
# range sum [l, r]:
# prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]

title: Strings — Operations
description: Strings are immutable sequences of characters in Python and Java — every modification creates a new string. Go strings are also immutable but slicing is O(1) without copying. Use StringBuilder (Java) or join (Python) for repeated concatenation.
diagram:
Concat in loop:   O(n²) — each + copies the whole string
  StringBuilder:    O(n)  — appends to a buffer, toString once
  ''.join(list):    O(n)  — single allocation

  Common ops:
  length      O(1)   slice/substr  O(n)
  find/index  O(n)   replace       O(n)
  split       O(n)   reverse       O(n)
keyPoints: Strings are immutable in Python, Go, and Java — use a list/builder for mutation
Python: ''.join(list) is the idiomatic way to build strings in a loop
Java: StringBuilder for repeated concatenation inside loops
Go: strings.Builder or []byte conversion for efficient building
Sliding window + frequency map solves most substring problems

solutions.python:
s = "Hello, World!"

# Length, access
print(len(s))        # 13
print(s[0])          # 'H'
print(s[-1])         # '!'

# Slice (new string)
print(s[7:12])       # 'World'
print(s[::-1])       # reversed

# Search
print('World' in s)  # True
print(s.find('o'))   # 4 (first index), -1 if not found
print(s.index('o'))  # 4, raises ValueError if not found

# Modify (returns new string)
print(s.lower())     # 'hello, world!'
print(s.upper())
print(s.strip())     # strip whitespace
print(s.replace('World', 'Python'))

# Split / join
words = s.split(', ')          # ['Hello', 'World!']
print(', '.join(words))        # 'Hello, World!'

# Efficient build
parts = []
for i in range(5):
    parts.append(str(i))
result = ''.join(parts)        # '01234'

# String ↔ list of chars
chars = list(s)                # mutable
chars[0] = 'h'
result = ''.join(chars)

# Palindrome check
def is_palindrome(s):
    s = s.lower().replace(' ', '')
    return s == s[::-1]

title: Maps — Operations
description: Maps (dict in Python, map in Go, HashMap in Java) store key-value pairs with average O(1) get/set/delete. Language-specific idioms differ — Python's .get(), Go's comma-ok, Java's getOrDefault/computeIfAbsent are essential to know.
diagram:
Operation         Python          Go              Java
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Set               d[k] = v        m[k] = v        m.put(k,v)
  Get               d[k]            m[k]            m.get(k)
  Get w/ default    d.get(k,def)    m[k] (+ ok)     m.getOrDefault(k,def)
  Check key         k in d          _, ok := m[k]   m.containsKey(k)
  Delete            del d[k]        delete(m,k)     m.remove(k)
  Iterate keys      for k in d      for k,v:=range  for (k,v):m.entrySet()
keyPoints: Python .get(key, default) never raises KeyError — prefer over direct access
Go: always use the comma-ok idiom (_ , ok := m[k]) to check existence
Java: getOrDefault, computeIfAbsent, and merge are the power tools
defaultdict (Python) / computeIfAbsent (Java) auto-initialises missing keys
Ordered iteration: Python dict preserves insertion order (3.7+); Go maps do NOT

solutions.python:
d = {"alice": 1, "bob": 2}

# Get — raises KeyError if missing
print(d["alice"])            # 1

# .get — safe, returns default
print(d.get("alice", 0))     # 1
print(d.get("carol", 0))     # 0  (no KeyError)

# Set
d["carol"] = 3

# Delete
del d["bob"]                 # KeyError if missing
d.pop("bob", None)           # safe pop with default

# Check
print("alice" in d)          # True

# Iterate
for k, v in d.items():       # key-value pairs
    print(k, v)
for k in d:                  # keys only
    pass
for v in d.values():         # values only
    pass

# defaultdict — auto-init missing keys
from collections import defaultdict
graph   = defaultdict(list)    # default: []
counter = defaultdict(int)     # default: 0
graph["a"].append("b")        # no KeyError
counter["x"] += 1

# Counter — frequency map shortcut
from collections import Counter
freq = Counter([1,2,2,3,3,3])
print(freq.most_common(2))   # [(3,3),(2,2)]
